feedback.text: I like to own what I pay for: music, video games, book... Unfortunately, I believe that we own less and less, since more and more companies can take back what we bought. For example, more and more companies shut down video games people bought for dubious reasons. When I say "shut down", it's not an exaggeration: it means that nobody can play it anymore, without even knowing that this was the fate of the game the company planned at the first place. There shouldn't be an invisible "limit date" on these products. I'm a software engineer for more than 15 years now. There are always ways to keep video games around, without shutting them down; after all, I still play 20 years old video games without any problem. The need and technology was different back then, but not that different: it's still possible today to offer an end-of-life for a video game, keeping it totally offline for the buyer to enjoy what they bought for as long as they want (or as long as they can make the video game work on newer technology). We need to stop this practice, by forcing companies to plan an end-of-life for their products. If the video game is mainly multiplayer, it could be a problem indeed; but most of them are not, offering a good single player experience which shouldn't be shut down. This is not asking companies to support their video games indefinitely. It's asking companies to stop the support, but let people still play the game. When I play a 20 years old game, I don't contact the company for them to make it work on newer technology; I make it work myself. When a company shut down a video game, it's impossible to run the game anywhere, and at any time.
